fontDescription: An OpenType font for the Rhineland neume notation used in Hildegard von Bingen's music manuscripts (Dendermonde Cod. 9 and Wiesbaden Riesencodex Hs. 2). Designed for use with a forked rism-digital/verovio as the shaping and staff-drawing engine, with MEI neume-module input. SMuFL-aligned where overlap exists; Hildegard-specific forms live in private PUA block U+F400-U+F4FF.

engravingDefaults.__comment: Values are placeholders; finalize after first .sfd pass. All in staff-space units (1 ss = distance between adjacent staff lines).

glyphsWithAnchors.__comment: Populated as .sfd glyphs are authored. v1 does not use GPOS mark attachment; v2 may use cutOut anchors for kern refinement.

glyphBBoxes.__comment: Populated from FontForge export. Each entry: { bBoxNE: [x, y], bBoxSW: [x, y] } in em-units (em = 2048).

glyphAdvanceWidths.__comment: Populated from FontForge export. Staff-space units.

hildegardExtension.description: Hildegard Rhineland neume private PUA allocations and paleographic provenance.

pueBlock.description: Hildegard Neume Private Extension — 256 codepoints reserved for Rhineland-specific forms not covered by SMuFL.

smuflReuse.__comment: SMuFL codepoints occupied by this font. Semantics match SMuFL; visual register is Rhineland.

U+E99B.smuflIntent: chantQuilisma — square-notation three-lobed form (Liber Usualis convention).
U+E99B.hildegardForm: Rhineland jagged-wave core attached to a smooth ascending tail. Never free-standing.
U+E99B.source: Gardiner 2022 pp. 94–95: 'two small jagged-looking waves with a gracefully curved line ascending the distance of a third or sometimes a fourth… connected to a smooth upward ascending stroke.'

privatePua.__comment: Hildegard-specific glyphs in PUA U+F400-U+F4FF. Each entry: { name, inventoryRef, description, paleographicSource }.

U+F400.name: hildegardDotPunctum
U+F400.description: Rhineland detached dot for climacus/scandicus chains. Visually distinct from chantPunctum at U+E990.
U+F400.paleographicSource: Gardiner Fig. 6 p. 91 (climacus, scandicus labels); ISHvBS editorial methodology at hildegard-society.org/p/music.html.

U+F401.name: hildegardTractulus
U+F401.paleographicSource: Humdrum Hildegard representation (https://www.humdrum.org/Humdrum/representations/hildegard.rep.html). NOT in Gardiner — verify against Dendermonde/Riesencodex before final commit.

U+F402.name: hildegardRoundedLiquescentTerminal
U+F402.description: Rounded, lighter-stroke liquescent ending shared across cephalicus, epiphonus, pes_liquescens, pressus_liquescens, and RN-022.
U+F402.paleographicSource: Gardiner 2022 p. 92: 'characterized by a rounded off, lighter stroke.'

U+F403.name: hildegardPressusCore
U+F403.description: Vertical line attached to a short undulating line. Shared across pressus, pressus_liquescens, pressus_subpunctis, RN-021, RN-022.
U+F403.paleographicSource: Gardiner 2022 p. 94: 'a vertical line attached to a short undulating line.'

U+F404.name: hildegardQuilismaJaggedCore
U+F404.description: Jagged-wave core of the quilisma. Never free-standing — always occurs attached to an ascending tail.

U+F405.name: hildegardAscendingTailConnector
U+F405.description: Smooth upward stroke used as quilisma tail.

U+F406.name: hildegardSubpunctisTail
U+F406.description: Descending chain of dot-puncta; variable length handled by renderer.

U+F407.name: hildegardTorculusRoundHead
U+F407.description: Round pen-drawn up-down head (for torculus rotundus).

U+F420.name: hildegardPes
U+F420.description: Two-note ascent. Span variants TBD (question § 4.1 in smufl_codepoint_mapping.md).

U+F421.name: hildegardPesFlexus
U+F421.description: Up-down three-note. Distinguished from flexa by slight uplift in tail.

U+F422.name: hildegardFlexa
U+F422.description: Two-note descent. Gardiner calls it 'clivis' in Fig. 6.

U+F423.name: hildegardFlexaResupina
U+F423.description: Down-up rebound. Gardiner treats porrectus and flexa resupina as synonyms.

U+F440.name: hildegardTorculusRotundus
U+F440.description: Round-headed up-down-up. Verify split against manuscripts — Humdrum-only distinction.
U+F440.paleographicSource: Humdrum Hildegard representation. NOT explicitly in Gardiner.

U+F441.name: hildegardTorculusQuadratus
U+F441.description: Angular up-down-up. Verify split against manuscripts.

U+F444.name: hildegardPorrectusFlexus
U+F444.paleographicSource: Inferred from standard chant paleography. NOT explicitly in Gardiner.

U+F460.name: hildegardCephalicus
U+F460.description: Descending two-note liquescent. Uses rounded-liquescent-terminal component.
U+F460.paleographicSource: Gardiner p. 60 — O viridissima virga, Ex. 9, Riesencodex f. 474v, word 'frumentum'.

U+F461.name: hildegardEpiphonus
U+F461.paleographicSource: Humdrum. NOT explicitly in Gardiner — verify against manuscripts.

U+F464.name: hildegardPesSubpunctis
U+F464.paleographicSource: Gardiner p. 82 ('subpunctis constructions', generic); Humdrum. Medium confidence.

U+F481.name: hildegardPressusLiquescens
U+F481.paleographicSource: Gardiner p. 60 (contrasted with cephalicus in O viridissima virga); pp. 93–94.

U+F4A0.name: hildegardFlexaResupinaPressusSubbipunctis
U+F4A0.description: Hildegard-idiosyncratic compound: flexa resupina + pressus core + subbipunctis tail. Dedicated glyph, not an assembly.
U+F4A0.paleographicSource: Gardiner p. 16: 'elaborate compound flexus respinus/pressus/subbipunctis' (sic — Gardiner writes 'respinus' without the 'u'; corrected to 'resupina' in production naming).

U+F4A1.name: hildegardFlexaResupinaPressusLiquescens
U+F4A1.description: Hildegard-idiosyncratic compound: flexa resupina + pressus core + rounded liquescent terminal. Dedicated glyph.
U+F4A1.paleographicSource: Gardiner p. 16: 'flexus respina/pressus liquescens' (sic). Flagged as 'idiosyncratic neumes… challenging to decipher.'

f.474v.description: O viridissima virga continued; cephalicus + pressus liquescens contrast (Gardiner Ex. 9, p. 60)

gardiner2022.citation: Gardiner, Katie. A Conductor's Guide to the Music of Hildegard von Bingen. DMA diss., Indiana University, 2022.

ishvbs.citation: International Society of Hildegard von Bingen Studies — Music page
